1:
"Paul, an apostle of Jesus Christ by the will of God, and Timothy our brother, unto the church of God which is at Corinth, with all the saints which are in all Achaia:" The witnesses note the apostolic salutation. Chrysostom asks why Paul adds a second epistle and why he begins with the mercies and consolation of God — having promised in the first to come, yet now writing again, he opens with comfort to soothe and prepare them. The witnesses note Paul opening the second letter as an apostle of Jesus Christ by the will of God, joining Timothy, and addressing the church at Corinth with all the saints in Achaia; the apostolic greeting that grounds his authority in God's will, associates a beloved fellow-worker, and embraces both the Corinthian church and the wider province; the apostolic salutation. The witnesses note an apostle by the will of God. The witnesses note the church at Corinth, with all the saints in Achaia.

The witnesses note the apostolic salutation — Paul an apostle by God's will, with Timothy, to the church at Corinth and all Achaia.

So the witnesses make the verse the apostolic salutation. The lesson the expositors draw is the grounding of Paul's authority in God's will, with the wide address to the church and province. The witnesses note Chrysostom's question: Chrysostom asks why Paul adds a second epistle and begins with the mercies and consolation of God; the question; the second epistle; the mercies; the consolation; the opening. The expositors observe the apostleship: "Paul, an apostle of Jesus Christ by the will of God" — Paul is an apostle by the will of God (not self-appointed, but called); the apostleship; the by God's will; the called; the authority; the office. The witnesses press the joining of Timothy: "and Timothy our brother" — Paul joins Timothy, a beloved fellow-worker; the joining; the Timothy; the brother; the fellow-worker; the associate. The expositors draw the lesson on the wide address: "unto the church of God which is at Corinth, with all the saints which are in all Achaia" — the letter addresses the Corinthian church and all the saints in Achaia; the wide address; the church; the all Achaia; the province; the saints. The witnesses observe the authority in God's will: Paul's authority rests in God's will — his apostleship divine, not human; the authority; the God's will; the divine; the apostleship; the calling. The expositors note the opening with comfort: Chrysostom notes Paul opens with the mercies and consolation of God — to soothe and prepare the Corinthians; the opening; the comfort; the mercies; the consolation; the soothe. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the an apostle by the will of God, the church at Corinth with all Achaia, and the apostolic salutation. The verse thus records the apostolic salutation — teaching the grounding of Paul's authority in God's will, with the wide address to the church and province.
2:
"Grace be to you and peace from God our Father, and from the Lord Jesus Christ." The witnesses note the apostolic benediction of grace and peace. Gill notes this salutation is the same as in the former epistle, and common to all Paul's epistles. Ellicott and Robertson note its identity with the standard Pauline greeting. The witnesses note Paul pronouncing grace and peace from God the Father and the Lord Jesus Christ; the customary apostolic blessing — that grace (God's free favour) and peace (the resulting tranquillity with God and conscience) are invoked upon the Corinthians, flowing jointly from the Father and the Lord Jesus Christ as one divine source; the benediction of grace and peace. The witnesses note grace be to you and peace. The witnesses note from God our Father, and the Lord Jesus Christ.

The witnesses note the benediction of grace and peace from the Father and the Lord Jesus Christ — the customary apostolic blessing.

So the witnesses make the verse the benediction of grace and peace. The lesson the expositors draw is the customary apostolic blessing, grace and peace flowing jointly from the Father and the Son. The witnesses note the grace and peace: "grace be to you and peace" — Gill notes this salutation is common to all Paul's epistles; the grace; the peace; the blessing; the benediction; the greeting. The expositors observe the grace: grace is God's free favour — the fountain of all blessing; the grace; the free favour; the fountain; the blessing; the gift. The witnesses press the peace: peace is the resulting tranquillity — with God, conscience, and others; the peace; the tranquillity; the with God; the conscience; the rest. The expositors draw the lesson on the joint source: "from God our Father, and from the Lord Jesus Christ" — grace and peace flow jointly from the Father and the Lord Jesus Christ; the joint source; the Father and the Son; the from both; the one source; the divine. The witnesses observe the Father and the Son as one source: the Father and the Son are named together as the one source of grace and peace — the deity of Christ implied; the one source; the Father and Son; the deity of Christ; the joint; the divine. The expositors note the customary blessing: the blessing is customary — Paul's standard greeting, yet full of meaning; the customary; the standard; the greeting; the meaning; the blessing. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the grace be to you and peace, the from God our Father and the Lord Jesus Christ, and the benediction. The verse thus records the benediction of grace and peace — teaching the customary apostolic blessing, grace and peace flowing jointly from the Father and the Son.
3:
"Blessed be God, even the Father of our Lord Jesus Christ, the Father of mercies, and the God of all comfort;" The witnesses note the doxology to the Father of mercies and God of all comfort. Gill notes God is blessed by men when they praise and glorify him; here he is described by his relation to Christ, and as the Father of mercies and the God of all comfort. Matthew Henry notes Paul begins the narrative of God's goodness to him and his fellow-labourers. The witnesses note Paul ascribing blessing to God, the Father of our Lord Jesus Christ, the Father of mercies, and the God of all comfort; the praise that names God by his tender titles — the source and fountain of all mercies and of every consolation, so that whatever comfort the afflicted receive flows from him as its spring; the Father of mercies and God of all comfort. The witnesses note the Father of mercies. The witnesses note the God of all comfort.

The witnesses note the doxology — God the Father of mercies and God of all comfort — the source and fountain of every consolation.

So the witnesses make the verse the doxology to the God of all comfort. The lesson the expositors draw is the praise of God as the source and fountain of all mercies and consolation. The witnesses note the blessing: "Blessed be God" — Gill notes God is blessed by men praising and glorifying him; the blessing; the praise; the glorify; the doxology; the ascription. The expositors observe the relation to Christ: "even the Father of our Lord Jesus Christ" — God is the Father of our Lord Jesus Christ; the relation; the Father of Christ; the of our Lord; the relation to Christ; the God. The witnesses press the Father of mercies: "the Father of mercies" — God is the Father of mercies (the source and parent of all mercy); the Father of mercies; the source; the mercy; the parent; the tender. The expositors draw the lesson on the God of all comfort: "the God of all comfort" — God is the God of all comfort (the fountain of every consolation); the God of all comfort; the fountain; the consolation; the comfort; the source. The witnesses observe the source of comfort: whatever comfort the afflicted receive flows from God as its spring — he is its source; the source; the comfort; the flows from God; the spring; the afflicted. The expositors note the tender titles: God is named by his tender titles — Father of mercies, God of all comfort — to encourage the afflicted; the tender titles; the Father of mercies; the God of comfort; the encourage; the afflicted. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the Father of mercies, the God of all comfort, and the doxology. The verse thus records the doxology to the God of all comfort — teaching the praise of God as the source and fountain of all mercies and consolation.
4:
"Who comforteth us in all our tribulation, that we may be able to comfort them which are in any trouble, by the comfort wherewith we ourselves are comforted of God." The witnesses note the purpose of comfort — that the comforted may comfort others. Gill notes Paul confirms the character of God as the God of all comfort by his own experience — that, though in great tribulation for Christ's sake, he and his fellow-ministers were not left destitute of divine support. The witnesses note Paul declaring that God comforts them in all tribulation, that they may comfort others by the comfort they themselves receive; the gracious economy of comfort — that God's consolation in affliction is not given for the sufferer alone, but equips him to console others in their troubles, the comforted becoming a channel of comfort; the comfort that overflows to others. The witnesses note comforteth us in all our tribulation. The witnesses note that we may be able to comfort others.

The witnesses note the purpose of comfort — that the comforted may comfort others — God's consolation equipping the sufferer to console.

So the witnesses make the verse the purpose of comfort. The lesson the expositors draw is the gracious economy by which God's comfort in affliction equips the sufferer to console others. The witnesses note the comfort in tribulation: "who comforteth us in all our tribulation" — Gill notes Paul, though in great tribulation, was comforted of God; the comfort; the tribulation; the divine support; the all tribulation; the comforted. The expositors observe the purpose: "that we may be able to comfort them which are in any trouble" — the comfort is given that they may comfort others in any trouble; the purpose; the comfort others; the in any trouble; the equipped; the channel. The witnesses press the means: "by the comfort wherewith we ourselves are comforted of God" — they comfort others by the comfort they themselves received from God; the means; the by the comfort; the received; the passed on; the same comfort. The expositors draw the lesson on the economy of comfort: God's consolation is not for the sufferer alone, but equips him to console others — the comforted becoming a channel; the economy; the not for self alone; the equips; the channel; the others. The witnesses observe the comfort overflowing: the comfort overflows — from God to the sufferer, and through him to others; the overflowing; the from God; the through the sufferer; the to others; the flow. The expositors note the experiential ministry: the minister, comforted in his own trials, can the better comfort others — experience fitting him for the ministry of consolation; the experiential; the minister; the own trials; the fits him; the consolation. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the comforteth us in all our tribulation, the that we may comfort others, and the comfort that overflows. The verse thus records the purpose of comfort — teaching the gracious economy by which God's comfort in affliction equips the sufferer to console others.
5:
"For as the sufferings of Christ abound in us, so our consolation also aboundeth by Christ." The witnesses note the proportion of suffering and consolation. Gill notes "the sufferings of Christ" are not those he suffered in his own person, but those his members suffer for his sake, said to abound because of their variety and greatness. The witnesses note Paul declaring that, as the sufferings of Christ abound in them, so their consolation also abounds by Christ; the gracious proportion — that the believer's sufferings for Christ (the afflictions endured for his sake, which Christ counts his own) are matched and exceeded by the consolation that flows through Christ, so that abounding suffering is met with abounding comfort; the sufferings and the consolation. The witnesses note the sufferings of Christ abound in us. The witnesses note our consolation aboundeth by Christ.

The witnesses note the proportion — as Christ's sufferings abound, so consolation abounds by Christ — abounding suffering met with abounding comfort.

So the witnesses make the verse the proportion of suffering and consolation. The lesson the expositors draw is the gracious proportion by which abounding suffering for Christ is matched by abounding comfort through Christ. The witnesses note the sufferings of Christ: "the sufferings of Christ abound in us" — Gill notes these are the sufferings his members endure for his sake, abounding in variety and greatness; the sufferings; the of Christ; the abound; the for his sake; the members. The expositors observe the meaning: the "sufferings of Christ" are not his personal sufferings, but those the believers endure for him — which Christ counts his own; the meaning; the not his person; the for him; the counts his own; the union. The witnesses press the consolation: "so our consolation also aboundeth by Christ" — so the consolation abounds by Christ; the consolation; the abounds; the by Christ; the comfort; the through him. The expositors draw the lesson on the proportion: the proportion is gracious — as the sufferings abound, so the consolation abounds; suffering met with comfort; the proportion; the as the sufferings; the so the consolation; the matched; the gracious. The witnesses observe the abounding comfort: the comfort abounds — matching and exceeding the suffering; the believer never comfortless; the abounding; the comfort; the matching; the exceeding; the never comfortless. The expositors note the comfort through Christ: the consolation flows "by Christ" — through him as the channel, the source of all comfort; the through Christ; the channel; the source; the by him; the comfort. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the sufferings of Christ abound in us, the consolation aboundeth by Christ, and the gracious proportion. The verse thus records the proportion of suffering and consolation — teaching that abounding suffering for Christ is matched by abounding comfort through Christ.
6:
"And whether we be afflicted, it is for your consolation and salvation, which is effectual in the enduring of the same sufferings which we also suffer: or whether we be comforted, it is for your consolation and salvation." The witnesses note that the apostles' afflictions and comfort serve the church. Chrysostom notes Paul lays down a second ground of comfort — that the disciples' salvation was procured by the apostles' sufferings: "faint not because we are afflicted, for this is rather a reason for your good cheer." The witnesses note Paul declaring that, whether afflicted or comforted, it is for the Corinthians' consolation and salvation; the selfless aim of the apostolic sufferings — that Paul's afflictions (and the comfort he received) were endured and granted not for himself, but for the church's good, his trials confirming the gospel and his endurance teaching them to endure; the afflictions for your consolation. The witnesses note afflicted... for your consolation and salvation. The witnesses note comforted... for your consolation.

The witnesses note the apostles' afflictions and comfort serving the church — Paul's trials endured not for himself but for the Corinthians' good.

So the witnesses make the verse the apostles' sufferings serving the church. The lesson the expositors draw is the selfless aim of the apostolic afflictions, endured for the church's consolation and salvation. The witnesses note the affliction: "whether we be afflicted, it is for your consolation and salvation" — Paul's affliction is for the Corinthians' consolation and salvation; the affliction; the for your consolation; the salvation; the for the church; the selfless. The expositors observe Chrysostom's note: Chrysostom notes the disciples' salvation was procured by the apostles' sufferings — "faint not, for this is rather a reason for your good cheer"; the procured; the sufferings; the faint not; the good cheer; the comfort. The witnesses press the effectual enduring: "which is effectual in the enduring of the same sufferings" — the consolation is effectual in their enduring the same sufferings; the effectual; the enduring; the same sufferings; the strengthens; the endurance. The expositors draw the lesson on the comfort for the church: "whether we be comforted, it is for your consolation and salvation" — the comfort too is for the church's consolation; the comfort; the for your consolation; the for the church; the salvation; the selfless. The witnesses observe the selfless aim: Paul's trials and comfort were not for himself, but for the church — his afflictions confirming the gospel, his endurance teaching them; the selfless aim; the not for self; the for the church; the confirming; the teaching. The expositors note the example of endurance: Paul's endurance teaches the Corinthians to endure the same sufferings — his example strengthening them; the example; the endurance; the teaches; the same sufferings; the strengthens. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the afflicted for your consolation and salvation, the comforted for your consolation, and the selfless aim. The verse thus records the apostles' sufferings serving the church — teaching the selfless aim of the apostolic afflictions, endured for the church's consolation and salvation.
7:
"And our hope of you is stedfast, knowing, that as ye are partakers of the sufferings, so shall ye be also of the consolation." The witnesses note the steadfast hope grounded in shared suffering and comfort. Gill notes Paul had long entertained hopes that the work of God was begun in them and would be carried on, and that they would hold on to the end, not moved by afflictions, rejoicing in hope of glory. The witnesses note Paul affirming that his hope of them is steadfast, knowing that as they share the sufferings, so they shall share the consolation; the confident assurance for the Corinthians — that Paul's hope for them is firm, resting on the certainty that those who partake of Christ's sufferings shall as surely partake of his consolation, the fellowship of suffering guaranteeing the fellowship of comfort; the steadfast hope. The witnesses note our hope of you is stedfast. The witnesses note partakers of the sufferings... and of the consolation.

The witnesses note the steadfast hope — as they share the sufferings, so shall they share the consolation; the fellowship of suffering guaranteeing comfort.

So the witnesses make the verse the steadfast hope grounded in shared suffering and comfort. The lesson the expositors draw is the confident assurance that those who partake of the sufferings shall partake of the consolation. The witnesses note the steadfast hope: "our hope of you is stedfast" — Gill notes Paul had firm hope that God's work in them would be carried on to the end; the steadfast hope; the firm; the carried on; the to the end; the confident. The expositors observe the knowledge: "knowing, that as ye are partakers of the sufferings" — Paul knows they are partakers of the sufferings; the knowing; the partakers; the sufferings; the shared; the fellowship. The witnesses press the consolation: "so shall ye be also of the consolation" — so they shall be partakers of the consolation; the consolation; the partakers; the shall be; the comfort; the shared. The expositors draw the lesson on the fellowship guaranteeing comfort: the fellowship of suffering guarantees the fellowship of comfort — those who suffer with Christ shall be comforted with him; the fellowship; the suffering; the guarantees; the comfort; the with Christ. The witnesses observe the confident assurance: Paul's hope is confident — resting on the certainty that the suffering and the comfort go together; the confident; the assurance; the certainty; the together; the hope. The expositors note the work carried on: Paul's hope rests on God's work in them being carried on to the end (Philippians 1:6); the work; the carried on; the to the end; the God's work; the assurance. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the our hope of you is stedfast, the partakers of the sufferings and the consolation, and the steadfast hope. The verse thus records the steadfast hope grounded in shared suffering and comfort — teaching the confident assurance that those who partake of the sufferings shall partake of the consolation.
8:
"For we would not, brethren, have you ignorant of our trouble which came to us in Asia, that we were pressed out of measure, above strength, insomuch that we despaired even of life:" The witnesses note Paul's account of his extreme trouble in Asia. Gill notes Paul desired they know of the trouble that lately befell them — partly to show his reason for thanksgiving, partly to encourage them to trust God in extremity. The witnesses note Paul telling the Corinthians of his trouble in Asia — pressed out of measure, above strength, so that he despaired even of life; the candid account of overwhelming affliction — that Paul, in some severe and deadly peril in Asia, was burdened beyond his own strength to the point of despairing of life itself, a trial so crushing that only God could sustain or deliver; the trouble in Asia. The witnesses note pressed out of measure, above strength. The witnesses note we despaired even of life.

The witnesses note Paul's extreme trouble in Asia — pressed beyond strength, despairing even of life — an affliction only God could sustain.

So the witnesses make the verse Paul's account of his trouble in Asia. The lesson the expositors draw is the candid account of overwhelming affliction, beyond his own strength, that only God could sustain. The witnesses note the disclosure: "we would not, brethren, have you ignorant of our trouble which came to us in Asia" — Gill notes Paul desired they know of the trouble; the disclosure; the not ignorant; the trouble; the in Asia; the made known. The expositors observe the pressure: "we were pressed out of measure, above strength" — Paul was pressed out of measure, above his strength; the pressure; the out of measure; the above strength; the overwhelmed; the crushed. The witnesses press the despair of life: "insomuch that we despaired even of life" — so that he despaired even of life; the despair; the of life; the deadly; the no hope; the extremity. The expositors draw the lesson on the overwhelming affliction: the affliction was overwhelming — beyond his own strength, deadly, so that only God could sustain or deliver; the overwhelming; the beyond strength; the deadly; the only God; the extremity. The witnesses observe the candid account: Paul candidly tells of his weakness — not hiding the crushing weight of the trial; the candid; the weakness; the not hiding; the crushing; the honest. The expositors note the purpose of the telling: Paul tells it to show his reason for thanksgiving and to encourage them to trust God in extremity; the purpose; the thanksgiving; the encourage; the trust God; the extremity. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the pressed out of measure above strength, the despaired even of life, and the trouble in Asia. The verse thus records Paul's account of his trouble in Asia — teaching the candid account of overwhelming affliction, beyond his own strength, that only God could sustain.
9:
"But we had the sentence of death in ourselves, that we should not trust in ourselves, but in God which raiseth the dead:" The witnesses note the purpose of the deadly trial — to teach trust in God. Gill notes the "sentence of death" was not a decree of God nor a magistrate's condemnation, but a persuasion in their own breasts that they should die — so far were they from hope of life, they looked on themselves as dead men. The witnesses note Paul declaring that they had the sentence of death in themselves, that they should not trust in themselves but in God who raises the dead; the divine design in the extremity — that God brought Paul to the very brink of death, where all self-confidence failed, precisely to wean him from trust in himself and cast him wholly on God, the God whose power to raise the dead is the ground of hope even in deadly peril; the sentence of death. The witnesses note the sentence of death in ourselves. The witnesses note trust... in God which raiseth the dead.

The witnesses note the deadly trial's purpose — to wean Paul from self-trust and cast him on God who raises the dead.

So the witnesses make the verse the purpose of the deadly trial. The lesson the expositors draw is that God brought Paul to the brink of death to wean him from self-trust and cast him wholly on God. The witnesses note the sentence of death: "we had the sentence of death in ourselves" — Gill notes this was a persuasion in their own breasts that they should die; the sentence; the death; the persuasion; the as dead men; the no hope. The expositors observe the purpose: "that we should not trust in ourselves" — the trial's purpose was that they should not trust in themselves; the purpose; the not self-trust; the weaned; the self-confidence failed; the design. The witnesses press the trust in God: "but in God which raiseth the dead" — but trust in God who raises the dead; the trust in God; the raiseth the dead; the power; the hope; the cast on God. The expositors draw the lesson on the divine design: God brought Paul to the brink of death precisely to wean him from self and cast him on God; the divine design; the brink of death; the weaned; the cast on God; the purpose. The witnesses observe the failing of self-confidence: at the brink, all self-confidence failed — only God remained; the failing; the self-confidence; the brink; the only God; the extremity. The expositors note the God who raises the dead: the ground of hope even in deadly peril is the God who raises the dead — his power reaching beyond death; the God who raises; the hope; the deadly peril; the power; the beyond death. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the sentence of death in ourselves, the trust in God which raiseth the dead, and the purpose of the trial. The verse thus records the purpose of the deadly trial — teaching that God brought Paul to the brink to wean him from self-trust and cast him on God who raises the dead.
10:
"Who delivered us from so great a death, and doth deliver: in whom we trust that he will yet deliver us;" The witnesses note the threefold deliverance — past, present, and future. Gill notes that, being enabled to trust God when all human help failed, the Lord appeared and delivered them from this heavy affliction, called a death because they were under its very sentence. The witnesses note Paul declaring that God delivered them from so great a death, does deliver, and in whom they trust will yet deliver; the confidence built on past mercy — that God's deliverance from the deadly peril (so great a death) is the ground of present and future trust, the experience of one rescue assuring the heart of rescues to come; the threefold deliverance. The witnesses note delivered us from so great a death. The witnesses note he will yet deliver us.

The witnesses note the threefold deliverance — God delivered, delivers, and will deliver — past mercy grounding present and future trust.

So the witnesses make the verse the threefold deliverance. The lesson the expositors draw is that God's past deliverance grounds present and future trust. The witnesses note the past deliverance: "who delivered us from so great a death" — Gill notes God appeared and delivered them from the deadly affliction; the past deliverance; the delivered; the so great a death; the rescued; the mercy. The expositors observe the present: "and doth deliver" — God does deliver (presently, continually); the present; the doth deliver; the continually; the ongoing; the mercy. The witnesses press the future: "in whom we trust that he will yet deliver us" — they trust God will yet deliver; the future; the will yet deliver; the trust; the confidence; the to come. The expositors draw the lesson on confidence from past mercy: past mercy grounds present and future trust — one rescue assuring the heart of rescues to come; the confidence; the past mercy; the present and future; the one rescue; the assurance. The witnesses observe the so great a death: the peril was "so great a death" — the very sentence of death under which they lay; the so great; the death; the sentence; the deadly; the extreme. The expositors note the trust in God: the trust rests on God — who delivered before, delivers now, and will deliver again; the trust; the on God; the delivered; the delivers; the will deliver. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the delivered us from so great a death, the he will yet deliver us, and the threefold deliverance. The verse thus records the threefold deliverance — teaching that God's past deliverance grounds present and future trust.
11:
"Ye also helping together by prayer for us, that for the gift bestowed upon us by the means of many persons thanks may be given by many on our behalf." The witnesses note the church's prayers as a means of Paul's deliverance. Gill notes that, though Paul ascribes the deliverance solely to God as its author, he takes notice of the saints' prayers as helping causes or means — a laudable practice, to pray for the ministers of the gospel, especially under affliction. The witnesses note Paul acknowledging that the Corinthians helped by their prayers, that the gift (deliverance) obtained through many might issue in thanksgiving by many; the value of intercessory prayer — that the prayers of the church were a real means of Paul's deliverance, and that the wide intercession would produce wide thanksgiving, the many who prayed joining the many who give thanks; the helping by prayer. The witnesses note ye also helping together by prayer. The witnesses note thanks may be given by many.

The witnesses note the church's prayers as a means of Paul's deliverance — wide intercession issuing in wide thanksgiving.

So the witnesses make the verse the church's prayers as a means of deliverance. The lesson the expositors draw is the value of intercessory prayer, the wide prayer issuing in wide thanksgiving. The witnesses note the helping: "ye also helping together by prayer for us" — Gill notes the saints' prayers were helping causes of the deliverance; the helping; the by prayer; the for us; the means; the intercession. The expositors observe God the author yet prayer the means: Gill notes Paul ascribes the deliverance to God as author, yet acknowledges prayer as a means; the God the author; the prayer the means; the both; the deliverance; the intercession. The witnesses press the gift: "that for the gift bestowed upon us by the means of many persons" — the deliverance (the gift) was obtained through many; the gift; the deliverance; the by many; the means; the obtained. The expositors draw the lesson on the wide thanksgiving: "thanks may be given by many on our behalf" — the wide intercession issues in wide thanksgiving; the many who prayed joining the many who thank; the wide thanksgiving; the many; the joined; the thanks; the praise. The witnesses observe the value of intercession: the prayers of the church were a real means of Paul's deliverance — intercession effectual; the value; the intercession; the real means; the effectual; the prayer. The expositors note the duty to pray for ministers: it is a laudable duty to pray for the ministers of the gospel, especially under affliction; the duty; the pray for ministers; the laudable; the affliction; the intercession. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the ye also helping together by prayer, the thanks may be given by many, and the helping by prayer. The verse thus records the church's prayers as a means of deliverance — teaching the value of intercessory prayer, the wide prayer issuing in wide thanksgiving.
12:
"For our rejoicing is this, the testimony of our conscience, that in simplicity and godly sincerity, not with fleshly wisdom, but by the grace of God, we have had our conversation in the world, and more abundantly to you-ward." The witnesses note the testimony of a good conscience as Paul's rejoicing. Chrysostom notes Paul opens yet another ground of comfort, exceeding great — lest the hearer grow supine, presuming only on God's mercy and others' prayers, he shows that they themselves contributed. The witnesses note Paul declaring that his rejoicing is the testimony of his conscience — that he had lived in simplicity and godly sincerity, not in fleshly wisdom, but by God's grace, especially toward the Corinthians; the clear conscience as a ground of joy — that Paul's confidence rests not in self-merit but in the witness of a sincere, single-hearted life lived by grace, his integrity vindicated against the slanders of his foes; the testimony of conscience. The witnesses note the testimony of our conscience. The witnesses note in simplicity and godly sincerity.

The witnesses note Paul's rejoicing — the testimony of a good conscience that he lived in sincerity by God's grace, not fleshly wisdom.

So the witnesses make the verse the testimony of a good conscience. The lesson the expositors draw is that Paul's rejoicing rests on the witness of a sincere life lived by grace, vindicating his integrity. The witnesses note the rejoicing: "our rejoicing is this, the testimony of our conscience" — Paul's rejoicing is the testimony of his conscience; the rejoicing; the testimony; the conscience; the witness; the joy. The expositors observe Chrysostom's note: Chrysostom notes Paul shows they themselves contributed (by integrity), lest they presume only on God's mercy; the contributed; the integrity; the not presume; the mercy; the ground. The witnesses press the simplicity and sincerity: "in simplicity and godly sincerity" — Paul lived in simplicity (singleness of heart) and godly sincerity; the simplicity; the sincerity; the single-hearted; the godly; the integrity. The expositors draw the lesson on grace not fleshly wisdom: "not with fleshly wisdom, but by the grace of God" — Paul lived not by fleshly wisdom (craft, cunning) but by God's grace; the not fleshly wisdom; the by grace; the not craft; the grace; the source. The witnesses observe the conversation in the world: "we have had our conversation in the world, and more abundantly to you-ward" — Paul so lived in the world, and especially toward the Corinthians; the conversation; the in the world; the to you-ward; the conduct; the life. 13:

15:
"And in this confidence I was minded to come unto you before, that ye might have a second benefit;" The witnesses note Paul's intended visit and its loving purpose. 17:

18:

20:
"For all the promises of God in him are yea, and in him Amen, unto the glory of God by us." The witnesses note that all God's promises are confirmed in Christ. 21:
"Now he which stablisheth us with you in Christ, and hath anointed us, is God;" The witnesses note God's work of establishing and anointing. 22:
"Who hath also sealed us, and given the earnest of the Spirit in our hearts." The witnesses note God's sealing and the earnest of the Spirit. Gill notes two more things are attributed to God — the sealing of his people (seals denoting property, distinction, esteem, security, and concealment), all expressive of God's grace in claiming and securing them. 23:
"Moreover I call God for a record upon my soul, that to spare you I came not as yet unto Corinth." The witnesses note Paul's oath that he stayed away to spare them. The witnesses note Paul calling God to witness upon his soul that it was to spare the Corinthians that he had not yet come to Corinth; the solemn oath revealing his tender motive — that Paul, who could have come in severity to punish their faults, stayed away on purpose out of love, to give them time to repent and be spared the rod, the delay a mercy not a slight; to spare you I came not. The witnesses note I call God for a record upon my soul. The witnesses note to spare you I came not as yet.

24:
"Not for that we have dominion over your faith, but are helpers of your joy: for by faith ye stand." The witnesses note the disclaimer of dominion and the ministry of joy. Gill notes that, having spoken of sparing them, lest it be thought he assumed a tyrannical power over the churches or lorded it over God's heritage, Paul subjoins this denial. The witnesses note Paul disclaiming dominion over their faith and professing instead to be a helper of their joy, since by faith they stand; the true character of gospel ministry — that the apostles do not lord it over the people's faith (which rests on God alone, by which they stand), but serve as fellow-workers for their joy, the minister a helper, not a master, of the believer's faith; not dominion, but helpers of joy. The expositors observe the not lording: the apostles do not lord it over God's heritage (1 Peter 5:3) — faith is not under their dominion; the not lording; the heritage; the faith; the not under dominion; the servants. The witnesses press the helpers of joy: "but are helpers of your joy" — instead, the apostles are helpers of their joy; the helpers; the joy; the fellow-workers; the servants; the ministry. The expositors draw the lesson on faith resting on God: "for by faith ye stand" — by faith they stand; faith rests on God alone, not the minister; the by faith; the stand; the rests on God; the not the minister; the faith. The witnesses observe the minister a helper: the minister is a helper, not a master, of the believer's faith — a fellow-worker for joy; the helper; the not a master; the fellow-worker; the joy; the servant. The expositors note the true character of ministry: the true character of gospel ministry is service, not domination — helping, not ruling, the faith; the true character; the service; the not domination; the helping; the ministry. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the not dominion over your faith, the helpers of your joy for by faith ye stand, and the true character of ministry. The verse thus records the disclaimer of dominion and the ministry of joy — teaching the true character of ministry, not lording over faith but serving as helpers of joy.
